citations.narrative:
In early 2024, the facility allowed members of the public to go inside the enclosure of an adult cougar with no barrier.
While no one was injured during this interaction, there is more than minimal risk of harm when allowing members of the
public to be in close contact with dangerous animals. The facility no longer allows members of the public to go in the
enclosure with the cougar.
At approximately 12 weeks of age, dangerous animals, including cougars and other big cats, become too big, too fast and
too strong to be used for public contact. During public exhibition, any animal must be handled so that there is minimal risk
of harm to the animal and to the public with sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing
public so as to assure the safety of animals and the public.
To be corrected from this day, May 22, 2026, forward.

An enclosure housing one bobcat is not being maintained in good repair to protect the animal from injury. In the indoor
portion of one bobcat’s enclosure, there is a plugged in electrical cord for the AC unit that the animal has damaged.
According to the facility representative, the cord is normally inside the wire cage holding the AC unit; however, at the time
of inspection, the bobcat had pulled it into the enclosure area and either clawed or chewed the outer covering.
Access to electrical cords can cause injury or death if clawed or chewed by the animals. All housing facilities must be
constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. All housing facilities shall be
structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals.
To be corrected by May 27, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.131(c)(1) 
   
Handling of animals. 
In early 2024, the facility allowed members of the public to go inside the enclosure of an adult cougar with no barrier. 
While no one was injured during this interaction, there is more than minimal risk of harm when allowing members of the 
public to be in close contact with dangerous animals. The facility no longer allows members of the public to go in the 
enclosure with the cougar. 
 
At approximately 12 weeks of age, dangerous animals, including cougars and other big cats, become too big, too fast and 
too strong to be used for public contact. During public exhibition, any animal must be handled so that there is minimal risk 
of harm to the animal and to the public with sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing 
public so as to assure the safety of animals and the public.  
To be corrected from this day, May 22, 2026, forward. 
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
An enclosure housing one bobcat is not being maintained in good repair to protect the animal from injury. In the indoor 
portion of one bobcat’s enclosure, there is a plugged in electrical cord for the AC unit that the animal has damaged. 
According to the facility representative, the cord is normally inside the wire cage holding the AC unit; however, at the time 
of inspection, the bobcat had pulled it into the enclosure area and either clawed or chewed the outer covering.  
 
Access to electrical cords can cause injury or death if clawed or chewed by the animals. All housing facilities must be 
constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. All housing facilities shall be 
structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals. 
To be corrected by May 27, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
